name: Cobra
tagline: One bite from this hooded hunter can drop an elephant—and it doesn't even want to fight you.
intro: When a cobra bites its prey, the snake hangs on to it for several seconds, grinding as much venom as possible into the wound. Cobra venom is extremely potent. A single bite from a king cobra could kill an elephant in just a few hours. Fortunately, cobras are not aggressive snakes. Most of the time, they just want to be left alone.

facts.label: How it kills
facts.value: Kills and defends by injecting or spraying venom

facts.label: Favorite meals
facts.value: Rodents, insects, small reptiles, and eggs

facts.label: Watch out
facts.value: When threatened, the cobra rises up, expands its hood, and shoots venom as far as 2.2 meters (7 ft) away!

topics.title: Spitting image
topics.text: Some African cobras have a very nasty habit. If they feel threatened, they spray jets of venom at their attacker, aiming for the eyes.

captions.label: Another squirter
captions.text: When frightened, the cane toad may squirt venom from the glands on its head. This toad has become a pest in Australia.

captions.label: Spray away
captions.text: The powerful jaw muscles of spitting cobras can spray venom quite a distance, with deadly accuracy.

topics.title: Convertible hood
topics.text: When cobras get angry, they raise the front of their bodies off the ground and spread out the bones in their neck, tightening the loose skin into a "hood" that's intended to scare off enemies. Some cobras grow twice as long as a compact car—those monsters might be able to raise their heads as high as yours!

captions.label: I can do that, too!
captions.text: This Australian frilled lizard also expands its neck frill to look more frightening to an attacker.

topics.title: Snacking on snakes
topics.text: Like all wild animals, snakes have to look out for predators that want to eat them. Birds and other snakes are their most common enemies.

captions.label: Snake-eating snake
captions.text: Farmers love king snakes, because they eat pests such as rats and mice. But they eat other snakes as well—rattlesnakes are one of the king snake's favorite meals.

captions.label: Snake-eating secretary
captions.text: The secretary bird walks for miles along the African savanna looking for such delicacies as cobra. Unlike other birds of prey, a secretary bird uses its feet to kill snakes.

topics.title: Dangerous babies?
topics.text: A snake doesn't have to be big to be deadly. A baby cobra can be as poisonous as an adult. Like all baby snakes, a tiny cobra is a miniature replica of its parents—in this case, the baby is ready to hunt the instant it breaks out of its eggshell.

captions.label: Testing the air
captions.text: A baby rat snake tests the air before emerging.

captions.label: Tiny egg, long snake
captions.text: It's amazing how such a long snake can come out of such a tiny egg!

topics.title: Milking a snake
topics.text: People sometimes "milk" venomous snakes like this rattlesnake. They make a snake bite a membrane-covered jar and then stroke its venom glands. The collected venom is used to make various medicines, especially antivenin for counteracting snakebites. Don't try this at home!

_provenance.note: Text liberated from original screen art; images & clip restored from disc. Original media is Microsoft/supplier copyright — placeholder pending swap to open-licensed assets.

video.title: Best of enemies
video.caption: Not many animals will mess with a cobra, but the mongoose is a worthy opponent. Although it's not immune, this little mammal can tolerate a dose of the snake's venom, and a mongoose is very quick. These two animals usually avoid each other, but when they get into a fight, the contest usually ends with the mongoose eating a dead cobra.
